content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                        Date : June 20, 1978


                                                A78-06-039

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Gina S-K

Subject: Telephone advice given to Alice Downs of the San Diego
         Police Officers Association Inc., (see letter dated May 12, 1978)


Ms. Downs asked whether or not contributions made by the San Diego
Police Officers'Association would qualify the Association as a
recipient or a major donor committee. Informed her that the
association is a recipient committee if it receives or expends
$500 or more of earmarked funds in a calendar year for political
purposes. They would not qualify as a major donor committee since
a history of making contributions has been established and the
members have reason to know that a certain portion of their dues
will be used for political purposes.

Informed her of registration requirement; requirement to file in
connection with elections and semi-annually when there is activity.

It is my understanding that the Secretary of State's Office has
already informed her of these requirements.




RSK:bw

SAN DIEGO POLICE OFFICERS ASSOCIATION INC.
Post Office Box 1630 • San Diego, California 92112

May 12, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gentlemen:

The Office of the Secretary of State has informed
us by telephone that we are required to file as a
"Recipient Committee"and forwarded to us the proper
forms therefor.

There is some question in our minds on the definition
of "Recipient Committee" as applies to our organization,
and we seek your help in ascertaining our proper position.

The San Diego Police Officers Association is comprised
of members who are sworn officers of the San Diego Police
Department, and is an employee organization. It is
maintained by dues from its members, no portion of which
dues have been designated as being for political purposes.
We are not a labor union.

You will note on the attached copy of sheet sent to us
by the Secretary of State that areas have been shaded
referring both to "Recipient Committee" and "Labor Union."
It would rather appear that our position is that bracketed
in red.

We respectfully request your interpretation on this. If
you wish to do it by telephone, our number is (714) 239-1283.

Sincerely,

Alice L. Downs
Office Manager

Affiliated with Peace Officers Research Association of California

CONTRIBUTIONS

EXAMPLES: REGULATION 18215

1. POLITICAL CLUBS

Political clubs normally receive dues from members. The dues
are generally not received exclusively for a political purpose*
but simply to maintain membership, pay overhead, conduct voter
registration drives. The raising of $500 for these purposes
does not make the political club a committee.

When the political club contributes $500 to a political campaign
or for a political purpose, it is a recipient committee if the
club traditionally contributes to candidates or ballot measure
campaigns. If it has never made contributions for a political
purpose, or if at the time of collecting dues members were not
advised that a portion of the dues would be used for political
purposes, the club will have a filing obligation only after
contributing $5,000.

If a political club makes a special fund appeal to members of
the public generally requesting contributions for a specific
political purpose, when $500 is received through this effort,
the club is a recipient committee because the money is earmarked*
for a political purpose.

2. COMMUNITY ORGANIZATIONS

A local organization called "Taxpayers for Reform" raises money
and makes expenditures to achieve the goal of property tax reform
in their community. It has never received or expended money in
support or opposition to a candidate or ballot measure or for
any other political purpose, nor has it received funds earmarked
for a political purpose.

"Taxpayers for Reform" would become a major donor committee when
it expends $5,000 for a political purpose, attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination
or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure.

3. LABOR ORGANIZATIONS

A labor union receives dues from members. The payment of dues
are made by members for union activities. The union then decides
to contribute to political campaigns. What must they file?

The original payment of dues was not for a political purpose nor
was a portion earmarked for later contribution, but because the
union has a history of making political contributions to candidates
and ballot measure campaigns, the union will become a recipient
committee after contributing $500 for political purposes.

*See regulation for definition of political purpose and earmarked.

sections.question: Ms. Downs asked whether or not contributions made by the San Diego Police Officers'Association would qualify the Association as a recipient or a major donor committee.
sections.conclusion: Informed her that the association is a recipient committee if it receives or expends $500 or more of earmarked funds in a calendar year for political purposes. Theywould not qualify as a major donor committee since a history of making contributions has been established and the members have reason to know that a certain portion of their dues will be used for political purposes. Informed her of registration requirement; requirement to file in connection with elections and semi-annually when there is activity.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the San Diego Police Officers Association qualifies as a recipient committee or major donor committee based on its contributions and use of member dues for political purposes.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The San Diego Police Officers Association qualifies as a recipient committee if it receives or expends $500 or more of earmarked funds annually for political purposes, but does not qualify as a major donor committee. Registration and filing requirements apply based on activity levels.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a telephone advice letter with both the incoming request (May 12, 1978 letter) and FPPC response (June 20, 1978 memo) included. The document includes regulatory reference material at the bottom regarding political clubs, community organizations, and labor organizations. High confidence in question and conclusion extraction; slight uncertainty due to some text corruption/OCR issues in the regulatory section.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides telephone advice to the San Diego Police Officers Association regarding whether it qualifies as a recipient committee or major donor committee under California political practices law. The FPPC advises that it is a recipient committee if earmarked funds of $500+ are used for political purposes annually, with applicable registration and filing requirements.
